meta.lessonTitle: How to Get Exactly What You Want From AI

steps.title: Welcome to Day 3
steps.body: Yesterday you learned which tools exist. Today you will learn the one skill that makes all of them useful: how to ask them exactly the right way. This is called prompt engineering, and it changes everything.

steps.title: The skill that unlocks AI
steps.body: Prompt engineering is not about fancy tricks. It is about giving AI enough context, a clear task, and a specific format so it knows exactly what you want. Vague request, vague answer. Specific request, useful answer.

steps.title: The four parts of a great prompt
steps.body: Every useful prompt has four pieces. Role: who the AI should be. Task: what you want it to do. Context: what it needs to know. Format: how the answer should look. Skip any of these and the answer gets weaker.

steps.title: Quick check
steps.question: What is prompt engineering?
options: Writing computer code to control AI
Learning secret keywords that unlock hidden AI features
Designing clear instructions so AI gives you useful, relevant results
Memorizing the longest possible prompt for every situation
feedback: Not quite — prompt engineering uses plain language, not code.
There are no secret keywords. It is about clarity, not tricks.
Exactly. It is the skill of giving AI the right detail to get the right answer.
Longer is not always better. It is about useful detail, not word count.

steps.title: Before and after
steps.prompt:
❌ Weak prompt:
"Write an email."

✅ Engineered prompt:
"You are a customer success manager. Write a follow-up email to a client who has not responded in two weeks. Keep it warm but professional, under 100 words, and end with a clear next step."

steps.title: Engineer your own prompt

fields.label: Who should the AI act as?
What do you want the AI to do?
fields.placeholder: help me rewrite a confusing paragraph

fields.label: What does the AI need to know?
fields.placeholder: this is for a team update, the audience is busy

steps.template: You are {{role}}. {{task}}. Here is the context: {{context}}. Please give me {{format}}.

steps.title: Copy your engineered prompt
steps.body: Now take the prompt you just built and paste it into ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or any AI tool you use.

steps.title: Paste what the AI gave you
steps.body: Paste the answer here. We will save it so you can compare how different prompts get different results.

steps.title: Good prompt or not?
steps.question: "Make it better."
feedback: Careful — this is too vague for AI to know what you mean.
Right. "Make it better" gives AI nothing to work with. Better or worse than what? Better how?

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: Adding a role like 'you are an expert marketing strategist' at the start of your prompt almost always improves the output.
feedback: It actually does help. Role-setting frames how the AI approaches your request.
Correct. Giving the AI a role narrows its focus and makes answers more relevant.

steps.title: Real talk
steps.body: You ask AI to write a project update. It gives you something generic and boring. The problem was not the tool — your prompt said "write a project update" with no details about the project, the audience, or what you wanted to emphasize. Three pieces of missing context made the difference between useless and useful.

questions.label: Think of a time you got a disappointing AI answer. What detail was missing from your prompt?
Rewrite that same request now using role, task, context, and format. How is it different?

steps.title: Day 3 complete
steps.body: You now know the core skill of prompt engineering: giving AI a clear role, a specific task, the context it needs, and the format you want. You built a real prompt and tested it. Tomorrow: how to chain prompts together to automate real workflows.
